claim: Output gap is theoretically incoherent (strong form)
reason: Contradicts the very inflation persistence both agents document. If potential GDP were purely demand-determined, there would be no supply constraints at any output level and inflation would never accelerate. The alternative conflict theory of inflation still requires capacity constraints — it relabels rather than eliminates the output gap. Weak form (output gap is poorly measured in real time with ±1.0-2.0pp revisions) is uncontested.

claim: Fiscal expansion base rate of 3-for-4 producing disorderly adjustment
reason: Causation is misattributed. The 1970 recession was driven by Fed tightening against Vietnam-era inflation, not fiscal adjustment. The 1987 crash was driven by portfolio insurance/market microstructure without recession. The 2008 crisis was driven by private sector leverage with the deficit at only 2-3% GDP. Fiscal deficits were present but not causal.

claim: IS curve permanently flattened by private credit rate insensitivity
reason: Private credit funds have 5-7 year lifecycles. If the next vintage raises less capital, the rate-insensitive pool shrinks. The current $200-300B/yr deployment may be a cyclical peak, not a structural floor. Directional claim survives as MODERATE_CONFIDENCE; permanent characterization does not.

claim: Expectations channel degradation based on survey-based inflation expectations
reason: 5Y5Y at 2.3% is 30bp above target — within measurement noise and TIPS liquidity premia. Michigan surveys are notoriously noisy with weak predictive power for actual inflation (Ang, Bekaert, Wei 2007). Modest drift does not constitute degradation.

Methodology is incoherent. Individual geopolitical probabilities (35-40% tariff escalation, 15-20% Middle East) have no empirical anchor. Compound probability depends on unspecified correlation structure. Directional claim (>15%) may be defensible; specific estimate is manufactured precision.

No disclosed methodology, no weighting scheme, no sensitivity analysis. Two-decimal precision on entirely subjective judgments creates an illusion of quantitative rigor. The ranking may be defensible; the specific scores are not.

claim: Scenario probability distribution (45% extended mid-cycle, 30% disinflationary, 15% stagflationary, 10% re-acceleration)
reason: Stated without derivation — no base rates, no model, no historical frequency analysis. Unfounded probabilities are worse than no probabilities because they create false confidence in positioning. The scenario framework structure is useful; the specific numbers are not.

claim: Credit-business cycle gap widest in at least two decades
reason: No measurement methodology provided. The directional claim (credit cycle more advanced than business cycle) is defensible based on structural evidence, but the superlative — wider than 2005-2007 when credit markets were demonstrably more extended — is unsupported and likely wrong.

claim: European growth model undergoing a geopolitically-forced structural shift
reason: The claim's own caveats effectively refute it. NATO 2% GDP defense target was set in 2006 and most members still haven't reached it 20 years later. European fiscal expansion has historically underdelivered relative to announcements. Aspirational, not analytical.
